title: SEGMENT 13 — Hemgenix — etranacogene dezaparvovec, the most expensive single dose

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Hemgenix — etranacogene dezaparvovec, the most expensive single dose
The third story is Hemgenix.
Etranacogene dezaparvovec.
Approved in November 2022 for the treatment of hemophilia B — the inherited bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor IX.
At the time of approval, Hemgenix took the title from Zolgensma as the most expensive single-dose drug in the world, launching at $3.5 million per dose.
Let me give you the disease context first.
Hemophilia B is an X-linked recessive disorder, so it predominantly affects males.
The gene that is broken is F9, which encodes factor IX, one of the proteins in the coagulation cascade.
Without factor IX, the blood does not clot properly.
Patients experience spontaneous bleeding into joints — which over time destroys those joints — and serious bleeding after even minor trauma.
The standard treatment for hemophilia B for decades has been intravenous infusions of factor IX concentrate, several times a week, for life.
The infusions work, but they are expensive — annual costs of $400,000 to $800,000 per patient are typical — and they require ongoing patient adherence.
The clinical promise of a gene therapy for hemophilia B is exactly the promise of converting a chronic, lifelong treatment regimen into a one-time intervention.
Deliver a healthy copy of F9 to hepatocytes — liver cells, which are the natural site of factor IX production — and have those cells produce factor IX for the rest of the patient's life.
No more weekly infusions.
No more chronic management.
One dose.
Permanent fix.
Hemgenix uses AAV5 as its vector, with a particularly potent liver-directed promoter driving expression of a high-activity variant of factor IX called the Padua variant.

The high-activity variant is an interesting wrinkle — it produces a factor IX protein with roughly eight-fold higher activity per molecule than the wild-type, which means you can achieve clinically meaningful clotting activity with less total protein production, which means a lower vector dose is needed.
The Padua variant was discovered in a single family in Italy whose members had unusually elevated clotting activity; that natural human variant became the engineered payload for a global drug.
The clinical trial supporting Hemgenix was a single-arm Phase 3 study in 54 patients with severe or moderately severe hemophilia B.
The endpoint was the annualized bleeding rate following treatment, compared to the patients' historical bleeding rates while on factor IX prophylaxis.
The trial showed substantial reductions in bleeding rates, with many patients able to discontinue prophylactic factor IX infusions entirely.
The agency approved the product.
The price — $3.5 million — provoked a second wave of the conversation that Zolgensma had started.

The justification was again largely actuarial: the lifetime cost of factor IX infusions for a hemophilia B patient is in the range of $20 to $30 million, so a one-time $3.5 million treatment is, in pure dollar terms, a substantial saving over the patient's lifetime.
The challenge is that the saving accrues over decades, while the cost is incurred upfront, which creates real cash flow problems for the payers who have to write the check today.
Hemgenix also illustrates another important feature of the gene therapy regulatory landscape: the role of competition and timing.
Hemgenix was not the first approved gene therapy for hemophilia.
Earlier products had been approved in Europe but had complex commercial trajectories.
The CSL Behring product that became Hemgenix was developed in part by uniQure, a Dutch biotech that had spent years optimizing the AAV5 platform.
When Hemgenix received FDA approval, it had a window of market exclusivity, but it knew that competing AAV-based hemophilia B therapies were also in development.
The pricing was set partly with an awareness that this window of exclusivity might be limited, and partly with the actuarial argument we discussed.

What Hemgenix demonstrated, regulatory-wise, was that the AAV liver-targeting approach — which had been talked about for two decades as the "next big thing" — had finally arrived as a commercially viable, FDA-approved modality.

The same AAV-to-liver framework that supports Hemgenix is now the basis for development programs targeting other liver-expressed proteins — Fabry disease, MPS II, hereditary angioedema, ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency, many others.
Each of those programs has, in its regulatory file, a pointer to the precedent that Hemgenix established.
